Sun Life Gains Full Ownership of BGO, Crescent Capital; Acquiring Bell Partners

 

Sun Life announced a series of multi-billion-dollar commercial real estate moves Monday that will impact businesses and properties on both sides of the Canada-U.S. border. 

 

Toronto-based Sun Life has acquired 100% ownership of BGO for $1.59 billion (US$1.16 billion) and Crescent Capital Group for $829M (US $608 million.) 

In addition, Sun Life has agreed to acquire Bell Partners, a leading U.S. multi-family real estate manager that has 70,000 apartments across the U.S. for US$350 million. Consequently, BGO and Bell Partners have agreed to merge and will represent more than US$100 billion of properties under management.

Subject: Re: Billable Hours Calulcations
 
Thanks Adam, 
Do you have any time on Wednesday?

Operating Partner
M 516.375.0621

From: Adam Nelson <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>
Sent: Friday, March 27, 2026 8:18:39 PM
To: David Pistacchio (Consultant) <David.Pistacchio@digitalbridge.com>
Cc: Sarah Blanchard <sblanchard@cloudops.com>
Subject: Billable Hours Calulcations
 
Dave,

Sarah mentioned you wanted some insight into the billable hours math I use. Happy to walk through the methodology, and I've included a couple of snapshots that show how it plays out in practice.

I don't use a flat 2,080 spread over the year. I build capacity bottom-up each month:

  1. Start with net working days in the month.
  2. Subtract planned vacations (a quick CSV export from ADP).
  3. Subtract company holidays. This is a bit more involved because of location nuances (UK, US, and each Canadian province has slightly different days off), but I only need to set it up once.
  4. That gives me workable time per person. I apply a utilization target by role: 80% for Professional Services engineers, 50% for Solution Architects.
  5. I total those up and add 15% capacity on top for SDM time, and that's our billable target for the month.

The reason I don't use 100% is that when engineers are rotating across 3-4 projects in a week, context switching makes it impossible to bill every hour. If they were embedded on a single engagement it would be different, but we don't have projects of that scale right now. 80% is a realistic, and a number that after 6 years here we seem to always end up at (and find is the sweet spot to plan around). 

Also, the reason I don't spread vacation evenly across 12 months is because a) it's usually noise, b) I have the data at my fingertips, might as well use it, and c) I need to plan delivery dates around weeks where someone is actually out, or where a company holiday takes the whole organization offline for a few days. This method gives me a much more accurate short-to-medium term forecast.


A few things to flag:
  • We are currently running at 86% utilization for March against an 80% target; currently over capacity.
  • Marcus and Nathan are fully committed to the Cofomo engagement (~300 hours/month between them). That engagement is a separate conversation, and I believe Sarah has given you some background. The short version is that at our $275 blended rate, those hours should be generating roughly $82K/month in PS revenue, but revenue was mostly captured on the front end, so unfortunately we are left capturing less revenue on the back end (hence the ~$38K). Overall on the project we are still above our blended rate, we just went way too hard early. Lesson learned. 
    • With those two committed to Cofomo, I effectively have Marc Hosang (plus Pat in a player/coach capacity) delivering everything else. That is roughly 4 equivalent delivery resources across ~20 active projects.
  • Tristan Kosciuch left the organization recently. We are backfilling with a more senior resource (architect level). Right now we need the team to be top-heavy. We don't have the luxury of carrying specialists who need constant architectural oversight from Pat. Marc Hosang is developing in that direction, but until the backfill happens, the bench is thin.
  • Where you see $0 in Rev. Delivered against planned revenue, that is typically a project where the original estimate (or a discounted rate) means we cannot recognize revenue above what the SOW allows. We still have to finish the work. 
  • This this the picture for March, as you can see we have ~20 projects in the hopper and with 2 of the 4 going almost full-time on one project that leaves Pat and Marc H to deliver the other 18. 


Happy to walk through any of this in more detail next week. Sarah mentioned you wanted to see the data before the weekend, so I wanted to get it over to you now.


Adam Nelson
Vice President, Operations

From: Sergio Bernardou <sbernardou@centrilogic.com>
Sent: Monday, March 30, 2026 11:51 AM
To: Marc-Alexandre.Forget@aptum.com <Marc-Alexandre.Forget@aptum.com>; Bradley.Petrie@aptum.com <Bradley.Petrie@aptum.com>; Adam.Nelson@aptum.com <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>; george.revie@aptum.com <george.revie@aptum.com>; Peter Bell <pbell@centrilogic.com>; Joshua Justice <jjustice@centrilogic.com>; Kevin Aplin <KAplin@centrilogic.com>; Karin Vierhuis <kvierhuis@centrilogic.com>; Mark Moore <MMoore@centrilogic.com>
Cc: Martin Mossakowski <mmossakowski@centrilogic.com>
Subject: Centrilogic & Aptum - Meeting Minutes – Bracknell update & Strategy - 26-03-30

 

Overview of Customer Footprint

  • The majority of customers are hosted in standard Centrilogic cabinets using shared power and carrier infrastructure.
  • One customer operates IBM mainframe equipment housed in non‑standard cabinets (wider and deeper), requiring confirmation of:
    • Power requirements
    • Weight and handling constraints
  • Only one customer (Perforce) operates within a dedicated cage (three cabinets plus expansion capacity).
  • Several customers also occupy office space in addition to data centre cabinets, which may influence their migration decisions.

 

Customer‑Specific Observations

  • Digital AI
    • Uses Centrilogic cabinets.
    • Requires physical separation between cabinets due to Wi‑Fi interference constraints.
  • Cloudian
    • Operates three cabinets with relatively high power consumption.
    • Also uses office space, though this is primarily for convenience.
    • It was agreed that, if migrating, the focus would likely be on rack space only, not office space.
  • Infinity
    • Uses one cabinet and office space.
    • Staff are onsite daily, making a migration to Portsmouth potentially less attractive.
  • OpenText
    • Uses six cabinets (five active, one for storage).
    • Makes frequent use of Smart Hands, which is included in their contract.
  • Perforce
    • Only cage customer at Bracknell.
    • Likely to seek an alternative location closer to their existing office, though requirements will be clarified in case migration is considered.
  • Telco providers (e.g., CityFibre, Colt)
    • Not expected to migrate to Aptum.
    • Will require time to remove or migrate their downstream customers from Bracknell.

 

Likelihood of Migration to Aptum

  • The general assessment is that more than 50% of customers may choose to migrate to Aptum.
  • A key differentiator will be the availability of migration assistance services, including:
    • De‑racking
    • Transportation
    • Re‑racking
    • Optional re‑cabling and Smart Hands support
  • Providing structured migration support is expected to significantly increase customer uptake.

 

Information Required to Progress

Aptum requested that the customer tracking spreadsheet include the following information for each customer: Number of cabinets and approximate REUs

  • Power provisioned and, where available, estimated power utilisation
  • Power feeds (A/B), number of circuits
  • Connectivity details:
    • Port speeds (1G, 10G)
    • Bandwidth utilisation
    • Use of Centrilogic carriers vs. customer‑owned circuits
  • IP address information:
    • IP blocks assigned (e.g. /29)
    • Confirmation of inclusion within the proposed /24 block
  • Contact status:
    • Whether the customer has already been contacted
    • By whom and when

 

Connectivity and IP Considerations

  • Most customers rely on Centrilogic carrier mix (Telia, LINX, Cogent, Virgin, Vodafone).
  • A small number of customers use customer‑owned last‑mile circuits (primarily BT or Virgin).
  • No major technical blockers were identified:
    • Aptum has sufficient space and power capacity.
    • Carrier compatibility appears feasible.
  • Follow‑up is required to:
    • Confirm power requirements for the IBM mainframe equipment.
    • Validate carrier mix and cross‑connect compatibility for migrating customers.
    • Confirm treatment of the /24 IP block for customer continuity.

 

Customer Communications

  • Two draft customer notification letters have been prepared but have not yet been sent.
  • Formal communication is intentionally being held until landlord agreements are finalised.
  • It was agreed that:
    • Aptum should not contact customers directly until a formal introduction is made by Centrilogic.
    • The draft letter will be shared with Aptum for review (already shared).
    • Aptum will contribute language describing:
      • Facility capabilities
      • Migration assistance services
  • The previously referenced June 30 date is not yet firm and remains subject to landlord agreement.

 

Key Agreements

  • No critical technical blockers were identified for the majority of customers.
  • Migration assistance is essential to the success of customer transitions.
  • The enriched customer spreadsheet is the critical dependency for next steps.
  • Early and coordinated communication will be key once formal notices are issued.

 

 

Action Items

  • Sergio B
    • Update and enrich the customer spreadsheet. - ONGOING
    • Share the spreadsheet and draft customer letter in the agreed shared folder. DONE
  • Aptum
    • Review the draft customer communication.
    • Provide proposed language covering facility details and migration services. @Bradley.Petrie@aptum.com,  @george.revie@aptum.com  would it be possible to receive the revised version by end of day today (3/30)?
  • Network & Facilities
    • Confirm power requirements for IBM mainframe equipment.
    • Validate carrier mix and cross‑connect compatibility.
  • All parties
    • Re‑engage once landlord agreements allow formal customer communication.

 

Please feel free to add any comments or suggestions.

SERGIO BERNARDOU

Project Management Consultant

 

sbernardou@centrilogic.com

www.centrilogic.com

Follow us: Linkedin | X

  • A key differentiator will be the availability of migration assistance services, including:
    • De‑racking
    • Transportation
    • Re‑racking
    • Optional re‑cabling and Smart Hands support
  • Providing structured migration support is expected to significantly increase customer uptake.

 

Information Required to Progress

Aptum requested that the customer tracking spreadsheet include the following information for each customer: Number of cabinets and approximate REUs

  • Power provisioned and, where available, estimated power utilisation
  • Power feeds (A/B), number of circuits
  • Connectivity details:
    • Port speeds (1G, 10G)
    • Bandwidth utilisation
    • Use of Centrilogic carriers vs. customer‑owned circuits
  • IP address information:
    • IP blocks assigned (e.g. /29)
    • Confirmation of inclusion within the proposed /24 block
  • Contact status:
    • Whether the customer has already been contacted
    • By whom and when

 

Connectivity and IP Considerations

  • Most customers rely on Centrilogic carrier mix (Telia, LINX, Cogent, Virgin, Vodafone).
  • A small number of customers use customer‑owned last‑mile circuits (primarily BT or Virgin).
  • No major technical blockers were identified:
    • Aptum has sufficient space and power capacity.
    • Carrier compatibility appears feasible.
  • Follow‑up is required to:
    • Confirm power requirements for the IBM mainframe equipment.
    • Validate carrier mix and cross‑connect compatibility for migrating customers.
    • Confirm treatment of the /24 IP block for customer continuity.

 

Customer Communications

  • Two draft customer notification letters have been prepared but have not yet been sent.
  • Formal communication is intentionally being held until landlord agreements are finalised.
  • It was agreed that:
    • Aptum should not contact customers directly until a formal introduction is made by Centrilogic.
    • The draft letter will be shared with Aptum for review (already shared).
    • Aptum will contribute language describing:
      • Facility capabilities
      • Migration assistance services
  • The previously referenced June 30 date is not yet firm and remains subject to landlord agreement.

 

Key Agreements

  • No critical technical blockers were identified for the majority of customers.
  • Migration assistance is essential to the success of customer transitions.
  • The enriched customer spreadsheet is the critical dependency for next steps.
  • Early and coordinated communication will be key once formal notices are issued.

 

 

Action Items

  • Sergio B
    • Update and enrich the customer spreadsheet. - ONGOING
    • Share the spreadsheet and draft customer letter in the agreed shared folder. DONE
  • Aptum
    • Review the draft customer communication.
    • Provide proposed language covering facility details and migration services. @Bradley.Petrie@aptum.com,  @george.revie@aptum.com  would it be possible to receive the revised version by end of day today (3/30)?
  • Network & Facilities
    • Confirm power requirements for IBM mainframe equipment.
    • Validate carrier mix and cross‑connect compatibility.
  • All parties
    • Re‑engage once landlord agreements allow formal customer communication.

 

Please feel free to add any comments or suggestions.

SERGIO BERNARDOU

Project Management Consultant

Zerto DRAAS Program – Operating Model

data.subject: Leyad Acquires Seven Loblaw-Leased Grocery Stores

body.content:
You don't often get email from newsroomcanada@connect.media. Learn why this is important
 

Leyad Acquires Seven Loblaw-Leased Grocery Stores

 

Leyad has acquired a 387,000-square-foot portfolio of seven single-tenant grocery stores located across Canada.

All of the stores are leased to Loblaw Companies Limited, one of Canada’s largest grocery-store operators. The portfolio spans British Columbia, Manitoba, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Saskatchewan and the Yukon, marking Leyad’s entry into both B.C. and the territory. Following the transaction, the company’s footprint now spans eight provinces and the Yukon.

Hi Adam,

 

If you write JavaScript or use Node.js, you need to check your environments right now. The official Axios package (which gets over 100 million weekly downloads) was hit by a critical supply chain attack between March 30 and March 31.

How the attack works (The Simple View)

This wasn't a typo-squatting attack where you accidentally install Axios. The attackers compromised the actual, official repository.

  1. The Breach: A hacker successfully hijacked the npm and GitHub accounts of the lead Axios maintainer.
  2. The Trap: They published two poisoned updates: versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4. Instead of altering the core Axios code (which would trigger alarms), they quietly added a malicious phantom dependency called plain-crypto-js.
  3. The Execution: The moment you run npm install, that hidden dependency triggers an nom postinstall script. This script automatically reaches out to a Command and Control server and downloads a Remote Access Trojan (RAT) built specifically for your operating system (Windows, Mac, or Linux).
  4. The Cover-Up: To hide their tracks, the malware immediately deletes the dropper script and restores a clean package.json file. Unless you caught it in the act, your codebase looks completely normal.

Network Chuk built an automated detection script to hunt for the IOCs.

If you are on Mac, Linux, WSL, or Termux, run this command in your terminal to safely scan your system:

curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theNetworkChuck/axios-attack-guide/main/check.sh | bash

(If you are on Windows using PowerShell, use this command instead:)

irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theNetworkChuck/axios-attack-guide/main/check.ps1 | iex
